flashcards.back: Big-O notation describes an upper bound on the growth rate of an algorithm. It represents the worst-case scenario, indicating that the algorithm's time complexity will not exceed a certain function as input size grows.

Big-Omega notation provides a lower bound on the growth rate of an algorithm. It represents the best-case scenario, indicating that the algorithm's time complexity will be at least a certain function as input size grows.

Big-Theta notation provides a tight bound, meaning the algorithm's growth rate is both upper and lower bounded by the same function. It indicates that the algorithm's time complexity grows asymptotically as a specific function.

flashcards.front: What is constant time complexity O(1)?
flashcards.back: O(1) represents constant time complexity where the algorithm takes the same amount of time regardless of input size. Example: accessing an element in an array by index.

flashcards.front: What is logarithmic time complexity O(log n)?
flashcards.back: O(log n) represents logarithmic time complexity where the algorithm's time increases logarithmically with input size. Example: binary search algorithm.

flashcards.front: What is linear time complexity O(n)?
flashcards.back: O(n) represents linear time complexity where the algorithm's time increases proportionally with input size. Example: linear search, traversing an array.

flashcards.front: What is linearithmic time complexity O(n log n)?
flashcards.back: O(n log n) represents time complexity that is the product of linear and logarithmic growth. Example: efficient sorting algorithms like Merge Sort and Heap Sort.

flashcards.front: What is quadratic time complexity O(n²)?
flashcards.back: O(n²) represents quadratic time complexity where execution time is proportional to the square of input size. Example: nested loops iterating over input, bubble sort.

flashcards.front: What is exponential time complexity O(2ⁿ)?
flashcards.back: O(2ⁿ) represents exponential time complexity where execution time doubles with each addition to input. Example: recursive calculation of Fibonacci numbers without memoization.

flashcards.front: What is factorial time complexity O(n!)?
flashcards.back: O(n!) represents factorial time complexity with extremely rapid growth. Example: generating all permutations of a string using recursion.
Little-o notation describes an upper bound that is not tight. f(n) = o(g(n)) means f(n) grows strictly slower than g(n) as n approaches infinity.
Little-omega notation describes a lower bound that is not tight. f(n) = ω(g(n)) means f(n) grows strictly faster than g(n) as n approaches infinity.
Space complexity measures the amount of extra memory an algorithm needs as a function of input size. It includes both the auxiliary space and the space used by input data.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between O, Ω, and Θ?
flashcards.back: If f(n) = Θ(g(n)), then f(n) = O(g(n)) AND f(n) = Ω(g(n)). Θ provides a tight bound while O provides upper bound and Ω provides lower bound.

flashcards.front: Why do we use asymptotic analysis?
flashcards.back: Asymptotic analysis allows us to compare algorithms independently of hardware, programming language, and input-specific factors by focusing on how performance scales with input size.
